steps.title: Meet the Parabola
steps.goal: See how the focus and directrix define the curve.
steps.info: Satellite dishes and car headlights are parabolic — their shape gathers incoming signals (or focuses outgoing light) at a single point called the focus.
steps.introText: A parabola is the set of all points equidistant from a fixed point (the focus) and a fixed line (the directrix). Changing either one reshapes the curve.
steps.prompt: Drag the focus, the directrix, or the point on the curve. Wherever the point sits, its distance to the focus (d₁) always equals its distance to the directrix (d₂).

steps.title: The Equation of a Parabola
steps.goal: Connect the geometry to the standard equation.
steps.info: Watch the coefficient: it's 4p — four times the focus distance — not p itself. That detail trips up a lot of people.
steps.introText: With the vertex at the origin, an upward-opening parabola follows x² = 4py. Here p is the distance from the vertex to the focus.
steps.prompt: Type at least three different values of p and press Try. Watch how each value reshapes the parabola and changes its equation.

steps.title: Match the Equation
steps.goal: Based on what you just learned, graph a parabola with equation x² = 14y

feedback.correct: Perfect! With p = 3.5, the equation is x² = 4(3.5)y = 14y.
feedback.incorrect: The equation isn't x² = 14y yet. Remember: 4p is the coefficient on y.
feedback.hint: 4p = 14, so p = 3.5. Place the focus 3.5 units above the vertex.

steps.title: Vertex Form
steps.goal: Shift the parabola by placing the vertex at a non-origin point.
steps.info: The general form for the equation of a parabola is (x − h)² = 4p(y − k). We still have the same paramater p, and we have two new parameters h and k. The point (h, k) gives us the vertex of the parabola.
steps.prompt: Place the vertex at (−2, 3) by moving the focus and directrix. While doing so, pay attention to how the equation of the parabola changes.

feedback.correct: Nice! The vertex is at (−2, 3) — the equation now includes (x + 2)² and (y − 3).
feedback.incorrect: The vertex isn't at (−2, 3) yet — adjust the focus and directrix.
feedback.hint: Move the focus to x = −2, then balance the focus height and directrix so the vertex sits at y = 3.

steps.title: Graph the Equation
steps.goal: Graph a parabola given its equation.

feedback.correct: Nice! (x − 2) and (y − 1) put the vertex at (2, 1), and 4p = 8 → p = 2, so the focus sits at (2, 3).
feedback.incorrect: Not yet — (x − 2) means h = 2, (y − 1) means k = 1, and 4p = 8 gives p = 2.
feedback.hint: Put the vertex at (2, 1), then place the focus 2 units above it at (2, 3).

steps.title: Downward Opening
steps.prompt: Graph a downward-opening parabola with p = -2 and vertex at the origin. Recall that a downward-opening parabola has a negative coefficient on y.

feedback.correct: Excellent! p = 2 below the vertex gives x² = −8y.
feedback.incorrect: Not quite — the focus should be below the vertex for a downward opening.
feedback.hint: Place the focus at (0, −2). The directrix should be at y = 2.

steps.title: Shifted Narrow Parabola
steps.prompt: Create a narrow upward-opening parabola with vertex at (−1, 2) and p ≤ 1.25.

feedback.correct: Well done! A small p keeps the curve narrow even when the vertex is shifted.
feedback.incorrect: Adjust the focus and directrix until the vertex is at (−1, 2) with a small p.
feedback.hint: Move the focus to x = −1, set the vertex height with the directrix, then place the focus about one unit above the vertex.

steps.title: Check Your Understanding
steps.prompt: Which statement is always true for a standard parabola?

choices.label: The vertex is at the origin.
The focus and directrix are equidistant from every point on the curve.
p equals the coefficient in front of y.

steps.feedback: As we learned, the focus–directrix distance property defines the parabola.

steps.title: Mastery Check
steps.goal: Construct four parabolas with specific width, direction, and position — no hints.

parabolaSequence.label: Wide downward-opening parabola at the origin
Narrow upward-opening parabola at the origin

steps.completionMessage: Congratulations! You now understand the basics of parabolas — including shifted and downward-opening cases.
